canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, computational efficiency is predominantly characterized as a model's ability to perform computations with minimal resource usage, commonly focusing on high speed and low consumption of memory, energy, or general compute. A very frequent framing, often labeled 'inference efficiency' or 'decoding efficiency', narrows this scope to the generation phase, emphasizing lower latency and higher throughput. Papers operationalize this construct through metrics such as speedup factors, with one study noting a "2.73× speedup" ("IceFormer: Accelerated Inference with Long-Sequence Transformers on CPUs"), reduced end-to-end latency, and increased throughput measured in "tokens per second per GPU" ("Puzzle: Distillation-Based NAS for Inference-Optimized LLMs"). Reductions in memory footprint and energy consumption are also common measures of this property. A smaller set of studies conceptualizes it as a balance or trade-off, for example, "considering compute-efficiency and performance trade-off" ("A Systematic Analysis of Base Model Choice for Reward Modeling") or weighing efficiency against other goals like safety. The construct is reported to be influenced by factors like `Activation sparsity` and `Token sparsity`, and it is also studied in relation to `Knowledge retention`.
expert_removed_reason: method/engineering — not a construct/measurement/behavior of LLM ability

definitions.text: The degree to which a model produces useful output with low latency and high throughput during generation.
The latent property of a model and its underlying hardware to perform computations with high speed and low energy consumption.
definitions.paper: DS-LLM: Leveraging Dynamical Systems to Enhance Both Training and Inference of Large Language Models

definitions.text: The degree to which a search procedure achieves a target performance using less test-time computation.
definitions.paper: Rewarding Progress: Scaling Automated Process Verifiers for LLM Reasoning

definitions.text: The degree to which an LLM can generate outputs with lower latency or higher throughput during decoding.
definitions.paper: PEARL: Parallel Speculative Decoding with Adaptive Draft Length

definitions.text: The degree to which a decoding method reduces the cost of generating tokens while maintaining output quality or target distribution matching.
definitions.paper: Multi-Draft Speculative Sampling: Canonical Decomposition and Theoretical Limits

definitions.text: The balance between model performance and the computational resources required for training and inference, allowing comparison across models of different sizes under similar compute budgets.
definitions.paper: A Systematic Analysis of Base Model Choice for Reward Modeling

definitions.text: The tendency of a model or decoding procedure to produce outputs quickly enough for real-time or near-real-time use.
definitions.paper: ReviewRL: Towards Automated Scientific Review withRL
